title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A regional public health authority has commissioned a report on the dietary habits of young people aged sixteen to twenty-five in your area. Your report should identify the main dietary patterns observed, analyse the factors contributing to poor nutrition in this age group, and recommend targeted interventions that schools, universities and local government could implement.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Dietary Habits of Young People Aged 16–25: Patterns, Causes and Interventions**

**Introduction**
This report analyses the dietary patterns of young people aged sixteen to twenty-five in the Greater Harwell region, based on a survey of one thousand eight hundred respondents and qualitative interviews with school and university nutrition coordinators. It identifies the primary drivers of poor nutrition and sets out targeted recommendations for institutional and governmental action.

**Dietary Patterns Observed**
Data reveal that approximately sixty percent of respondents consume fewer than the recommended five portions of fruit and vegetables per day. Consumption of ultra-processed foods — including fast food, packaged snacks and energy drinks — is highest in the sixteen-to-nineteen age cohort, with frequency correlated positively with lower household income. Meal skipping, particularly breakfast, was reported by forty-three percent of respondents in full-time education.

**Contributing Factors**
Three factors predominate: limited cooking skills and nutritional knowledge, reported by over half of participants; the low cost and high availability of energy-dense, nutritionally poor foods near educational institutions; and time pressure associated with part-time employment alongside study.

**Recommendations**
Three evidence-based interventions are recommended. Secondary schools should incorporate practical cooking skills into the curriculum as a statutory requirement. Universities should expand subsidised hot meal provision and partner with student unions on peer-led nutritional awareness campaigns. Local authorities should restrict fast-food outlet licensing within five hundred metres of educational establishments.

**Conclusion**
Improving young people's dietary habits requires a coordinated response across educational, commercial and regulatory domains, with particular attention to the structural inequalities that constrain food choice.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You are a parent and member of a school governing body. You have become concerned that the school canteen consistently offers meals that are high in sugar, salt and processed ingredients, and that healthy options are limited and unpopular with students. Write an email to the school's headteacher expressing your concerns, outlining specific changes you would like to see, and proposing that the governing body discuss the issue at its next meeting.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Ms Okonkwo,

I am writing in my capacity as a parent governor to raise concerns about the current standard of food provision in the school canteen, a matter I believe warrants consideration by the full governing body at our next scheduled meeting.

Over the past term, I have had the opportunity to review the weekly canteen menus and to speak informally with a number of students about their lunchtime choices. The picture that emerges is, I think, worrying. The majority of hot meal options are heavily reliant on processed ingredients, with a high salt and saturated fat content, while fresh fruit, salad and vegetables are available only in limited quantities and are consistently less popular — in part, students tell me, because of presentation and pricing relative to less nutritious alternatives.

I fully appreciate that school catering operates under considerable budgetary constraints, and I raise this not to assign blame but because I believe the issue has a meaningful impact on students' concentration, energy levels and long-term health habits. Research on the relationship between school meal quality and academic performance is, I think, sufficiently robust to justify treating this as an educational as well as a nutritional concern.

I would like to propose three specific areas for discussion: a review of the current catering contract against nutritional standards benchmarks; consultation with students on barriers to choosing healthier options; and exploration of whether external funding — through, for example, the School Fruit and Vegetable Scheme — could supplement existing provision.

I look forward to discussing this further.

Best regards,
David Carrington
Parent Governor

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a book called 'Eat Well, Live Well: The Case for a Food Revolution' which argues that Western dietary patterns are the principal driver of a global chronic disease crisis, and that radical food system reform — including taxation of ultra-processed foods, subsidies for fresh produce, and mandatory nutritional education — is both necessary and achievable. Write a review for a public health journal evaluating the rigour of the book's arguments, the strength of its evidence, and the feasibility of its policy proposals.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Compelling Diagnosis, Uneven Prescription: A Review of 'Eat Well, Live Well: The Case for a Food Revolution'**

In 'Eat Well, Live Well', nutritional epidemiologist Dr Saoirse Brennan sets out a detailed and passionately argued case that the chronic disease burden afflicting Western populations — from type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease to certain cancers — is substantially attributable to dietary patterns characterised by high consumption of ultra-processed, nutrient-poor foods, and that the food systems producing these patterns are amenable to policy intervention.

The book's diagnostic sections are its strongest. Brennan marshals an impressive body of longitudinal epidemiological evidence, including the NOVA classification framework studies and data from the EPIC cohort, to establish the association between ultra-processed food consumption and adverse health outcomes with admirable rigour. She is careful to distinguish correlation from causation where the evidence demands it, lending her argument a credibility that more polemical accounts in this genre often lack.

The policy proposals — fiscal measures targeting ultra-processed products, subsidies for fresh produce, statutory nutritional education — are individually well-evidenced by international precedent, drawing on Mexico's sugar tax experience and Denmark's experiments with fat taxation. However, Brennan is less convincing when addressing implementation barriers, particularly the political economy of food industry lobbying and the regressive distributional effects of consumption taxes, which receive relatively brief treatment.

Nevertheless, 'Eat Well, Live Well' is a valuable and timely contribution to the public health literature. It is required reading for anyone engaged seriously with food policy, even where its prescriptions invite productive disagreement.
